a: RAG is a way to make a language model answer using your own documents. Before the model writes its answer, you fetch relevant text and paste it into the prompt, so the model reads real facts instead of guessing from memory.
fs: RAG = retrieve relevant text first, then generate an answer grounded in that text, instead of relying on the model's trained-in memory alone.
fj: Thinking RAG retrains or fine-tunes the model on your data, when it actually just feeds documents in at question time.

a: Retrieval is the step that searches your collection of documents and pulls back the few pieces most related to the user's question. Those pieces are what the model gets to read before answering.
fs: Retrieval finds the top-k most relevant chunks for a query, usually by similarity search, so only useful context reaches the model.
fj: Assuming retrieval sends every document to the model; in reality it returns just the few best matches, and bad retrieval means a bad answer.

q: What is a chunk in RAG?
a: A chunk is a small slice of a document, like a paragraph or a few sentences. You split big documents into chunks so retrieval can return just the relevant part instead of a whole 50-page file.
fs: A chunk is a bite-sized, separately searchable piece of a document; chunk size trades off precise matching against keeping enough context together.
fj: Making chunks too big (whole files) or too tiny (single lines), which either buries the answer or splits it across pieces.

q: What is an embedding in RAG?
a: An embedding turns a piece of text into a list of numbers that captures its meaning. Texts about similar topics end up with similar numbers, which lets the computer find related chunks by comparing them mathematically.
fs: An embedding is a vector representation of text's meaning; similarity (e.g. cosine) between vectors powers semantic retrieval beyond exact keyword matches.
fj: Confusing embedding similarity with keyword matching, expecting hits only when the exact words appear instead of when the meaning is close.

a: Grounding means the model bases its answer on the retrieved text you gave it, not on guesswork. You stuff those chunks into the prompt as context, and ask the model to answer only from them, which cuts down made-up answers.
fs: Grounding constrains generation to the retrieved context so claims trace back to real sources, reducing hallucination and enabling citations.
fj: Believing grounding makes hallucination impossible; if retrieval misses or the model ignores the context, it can still answer wrongly.
